request.question: Which of Demetrius Calip's 1988-1989 National Championship team's members is currently serving as the general manager of another sports team?

title: Michigan State Spartans men's basketball
William C. "King" Cole
Demetrius Calip
Doug Gilmour
George Terlep
Rich Cho
1989 Mongolian National Championship
Jay Feaster
High School Football National Championship
Rob Pelinka
sentences: The Michigan State Spartans men's basketball team represents Michigan State University (MSU) and competes in the Big Ten Conference of NCAA Division I College basketball.
Their home games are played at the Breslin Student Events Center.
Tom Izzo has been the head coach since 1995.
The Spartans have won two NCAA championships and 13 Big Ten Conference Championships.
Their two National Championships came in the 1979 NCAA Tournament and the 2000 NCAA Tournament.
The 1979 National Championship Game was the most watched college basketball game in history, with 35.11 million television viewers.
The 1979 National Championship team was coached by Jud Heathcote and included tournament MVP Magic Johnson, Greg Kelser and Jay Vincent.
The Spartans defeated the previously unbeaten Indiana State Sycamores, led by future Hall of Famer Larry Bird.
The 2000 National Championship team defeated the Florida Gators in the final.
The team was coached by Tom Izzo and led by players Morris Peterson, Charlie Bell, Jason Richardson and tournament MVP Mateen Cleaves.
William Cutler "King" Cole (October 7, 1881 – April 23, 1968) was a college football player and coach.
He played as a tackle and end for the University of Michigan's 1902 "Point-a-Minute" championship football team.
He played for an undefeated national championship team at Michigan in 1902 and was assistant coach to Fielding H. Yost for a second undefeated national championship team in 1904.
He later became the head football coach at Marietta College (1903), University of Virginia (1905–1906), and University of Nebraska (1907–1910).
He led the Nebraska Cornhuskers to two Missouri Valley Intercollegiate Athletic Association championships.
Demetrius Calip (born November 18, 1969) is an American professional basketball player formerly of the Los Angeles Lakers of the NBA.
Born in Flint, Michigan, he helped the University of Michigan Wolverines to the 1989 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship.
As a member of the 1988-1989 National Champions, his teammates included Glen Rice, Terry Mills, Loy Vaught, Rumeal Robinson, Sean Higgins, and Rob Pelinka.
As a member of the 1990-91 team he led the team in scoring, assists and minutes.
Other University of Michigan Wolverine teammates included Eric Riley and Gary Grant.
And was coached by grad assistant Joe Czupek
Douglas Robert Gilmour (born June 25, 1963) is a Canadian former professional ice hockey player and current general manager of the Kingston Frontenacs of the Ontario Hockey League (OHL).
He played 20 seasons in the National Hockey League (NHL) for the St. Louis Blues, Calgary Flames, Toronto Maple Leafs, New Jersey Devils, Chicago Blackhawks, Buffalo Sabres and Montreal Canadiens.
Gilmour was a seventh round selection, 134th overall, of the Blues at the 1982 NHL Entry Draft and recorded 1,414 points in 1,474 games in the NHL between 1983 and 2003.
A two-time All-Star, he was a member of Calgary's 1989 Stanley Cup championship team and won the Frank J. Selke Trophy as the NHL's best defensive forward in 1992–93.
Internationally, he represented Canada three times during his career and was a member of the nation's 1987 Canada Cup championship team.

George Rudolph "Duke" Terlep (April 12, 1923 – May 17, 2010) was an American football player, coach, and general manager who was on a college national championship team at Notre Dame in 1943 and won another championship while playing for the Cleveland Browns in the All-America Football Conference (AAFC) in 1948.
Terlep also won two Grey Cup championships in the Canadian Football League (CFL), once as an assistant with the Hamilton Tiger-Cats and once as the general manager of the Ottawa Rough Riders.

Richard Cho (born August 10, 1965), more commonly known by his nickname "Trader Cho" is a Burmese American basketball executive, currently serving as the general manager of the Charlotte Hornets of the National Basketball Association.
Prior to the Hornets, Cho was the general manager of the Portland Trail Blazers and the assistant general manager of the Oklahoma City Thunder.
Cho is the first Asian American general manager in NBA history.

The 1989 Mongolian National Championship was the twenty-fifth recorded edition of the Mongolian National Championship for football, with the first tournament taking place in 1955 and no tournament held in 1965 or apparently in 1977.

It would appear however that championships were contested between 1956 and 1963, as sources note that a team called Aldar, the Mongolian Army Sports Club, won the title on numerous occasions during that time.

Nonetheless, the 1989 national championship was won for the fourth time by Khuldumur (literally: "Labour" or "Workers" and also romanised as Hödölmör) and the first time since their success in the 1972 season

Jay Harry Feaster (born July 30, 1962 in Harrisburg, Pennsylvania) is a National Hockey League (NHL) executive currently serving as the Executive Director of Community Hockey Development for the Tampa Bay Lightning.

He is the former general manager of the Calgary Flames, having served from May 16, 2011 to December 12, 2013, after serving as acting general manager since December 28, 2010, following Darryl Sutter's resignation.

He was the general manager of the Tampa Bay Lightning for six years, during which he was named the NHL's executive of the year by "The Sporting News" in 2004 after guiding the Lightning to their first Stanley Cup championship.

The High School Football National Championship is a national championship honor awarded to the best high school football team(s) in the United States of America based on rankings from "USA Today" and the National Prep Poll.
There have been some efforts over the years at organizing a single-game playoff for the national championship.
Sometimes a dominant team in one state would defeat a dominant team in a neighboring state after the regular season and then would self-claim the national championship.

However, sometimes such a game could not be scheduled, like in 1936 after Washington High School of Massillon, Ohio refused to withhold its black players in a proposed game with segregated Central High School of Knoxville, Tennessee.
Central High subsequently proclaimed itself national champion that year.

On December 31, 1938, duPont Manual of Louisville, Kentucky and New Britain of Connecticut played in an actual national championship game in Baton Rouge with the Louisiana Sports Association as the formal sponsor – and, by extension, the Sugar Bowl Committee, which held a series of sporting events leading up to the Sugar Bowl game itself.
Manual won, 28–20.
The following year, on December 30, the game featured Pine Bluff, Arkansas, which defeated Baton Rouge High School by a score of 26–0.
This series of games proved difficult to organize, due to some states' prohibition of postseason play.
Pine Bluff, for example, had to receive a special waiver from its state to participate in the game.
In 1962, Florida state champion Miami Senior High beat Baltimore Polytechnic in the Orange Bowl and was recognized by Imperial Sports Syndicate of California as a national champion.

Robert Todd Pelinka Jr. (born December 23, 1969) is an American lawyer, National Basketball Association (NBA) team executive, sports agent, and former college basketball player from Lake Bluff, Illinois (suburban Chicago).
Pelinka is currently the general manager of the Los Angeles Lakers.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: Which of Demetrius Calip's 1988-1989 National Championship team's members is currently serving as the general manager of another sports team?
hop1_docs: Demetrius Calip | Demetrius Calip (born November 18, 1969) is an American professional basketball player formerly of the Los Angeles Lakers of the NBA.  Born in Flint, Michigan, he helped the University of Michigan Wolverines to the 1989 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship.  As a member of the 1988-1989 National Champions, his teammates included Glen Rice, Terry Mills, Loy Vaught, Rumeal Robinson, Sean Higgins, and Rob Pelinka.  As a member of the 1990-91 team he led the team in scoring, assists and minutes.  Other University of Michigan Wolverine teammates included Eric Riley and Gary Grant.  And was coached by grad assistant Joe Czupek

Caribbean Cup | The Caribbean Cup is the championship tournament for national association football teams that are members of the Caribbean Football Union.  The first competition was contested in 1989 in Barbados.  The Caribbean Cup serves as a qualification tournament among CFU members for the CONCACAF Gold Cup.  The Caribbean Cup replaced the CFU Championship competition which was active between 1978 and 1988.

List of Nashville Sounds managers and owners | The Nashville Sounds Minor League Baseball team has employed 27 managers and 9 general managers since its establishment in Nashville, Tennessee, in the 1978 season.  Three managers have guided the team to win their league's championship.  George Scherger led the team to win the Southern League Championship in the 1979 season as the Double-A affiliate of the Cincinnati Reds.  Johnny Oates managed the team to another Southern League title in 1982 as the Double-A affiliate of the New York Yankees.  In the 2005 season, as the Triple-A affiliate of the Milwaukee Brewers, Frank Kremblas led the team to win the Pacific Coast League Championship.  Ryan Christenson is the team's current manager.

Alexander Koreshkov (ice hockey) | Alexander Gennadievich Koreshkov (Russian: Александр Геннадьевич Корешков ; born 28 October 1968) is a retired Kazakhstani professional ice hockey left winger who last competed at the 2010 IIHF World Championship as a member of the Kazakhstan men's national ice hockey team.  He is the older brother of Evgeni Koreshkov.  He is currently serves as a president of the Kontinental Hockey League team Barys Astana and a general manager of the Kazakhstan men's national ice hockey team.

Kaz Patafta | Kaz Phonesak Patafta (born 25 October 1988) is the current General Manager for Lane Xang Intra F.C. in Laos.  He was formerly an Australian professional footballer.  Patafta previously played as a midfielder for Benfica, Melbourne Victory, Newcastle Jets and Khon Kaen United F.C..  He captained the Australia under-17 national team at the 2005 FIFA U-17 World Championship in Peru and also the Australia under-20 national team at the 2006 AFC Youth Championship.  Current Australian national team coach, Ange Postecoglou, famously declared Patafta as "the most technically gifted player I've coached."

List of Colorado Avalanche head coaches | The Colorado Avalanche are an American professional ice hockey team based in Denver, Colorado.  They play in the Central Division of the Western Conference in the National Hockey League (NHL).  The team joined the NHL in 1972 as a charter member of the World Hockey Association, and were named the Quebec Nordiques, but moved to Denver in 1995.  The Avalanche won their first Stanley Cup championship in 1996, and won another one in 2001.  Having first played at the McNichols Sports Arena, the Avalanche have played their home games at Pepsi Center since 1999.  The Avalanche are owned by Stan Kroenke, Greg Sherman is their general manager, and Gabriel Landeskog is the team captain.

Demetrius Brown | Demetrius Brown (born c. 1967) is a former American football player.  He was a quarterback for the University of Michigan Wolverines football team from 1985 to 1989 and a starting quarterback for the 1987 and 1988 teams.  Brown was the first Michigan quarterback to lead the Wolverines to bowl victories in consecutive seasons, with victories over Alabama in the 1988 Hall of Fame Bowl and USC in the 1989 Rose Bowl.  During the 1987 season, Brown also set Michigan school records with seven interceptions in a game and 16 interceptions in a season.
hop1_titles: List of Nashville Sounds managers and owners
List of Colorado Avalanche head coaches
response.summary_1:
Primary topic: Demetrius Calip and his 1988–1989 University of Michigan national-championship teammates.

- Fact (from passage 1): Demetrius Calip "helped the University of Michigan Wolverines to the 1989 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship."
- Fact (from passage 1): "As a member of the 1988-1989 National Champions, his teammates included" the named players: Glen Rice; Terry Mills; Loy Vaught; Rumeal Robinson; Sean Higgins; Rob Pelinka.
- Missing fact / ambiguity: The passages do NOT state which (if any) of these teammates "is currently serving as the general manager of another sports team." Bridge entities to resolve: Glen Rice; Terry Mills; Loy Vaught; Rumeal Robinson; Sean Higgins; Rob Pelinka.
- Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
  1. Rob Pelinka — fetch page "Rob Pelinka" (lede/infobox and section on "Executive career" / "Front office") or search "Rob Pelinka general manager Los Angeles Lakers".
  2. Glen Rice — fetch "Glen Rice" (lede/infobox; "Post-playing career" or "Coaching/Executive" sections).
  3. Terry Mills; Loy Vaught; Rumeal Robinson; Sean Higgins — fetch each player page (lede/infobox and "Post-playing career"/"Coaching/Executive" sections).
  4. If needed, fetch team executive lists (e.g., "List of Los Angeles Lakers executives" or relevant team front-office pages) to confirm any GM appointments.
hop2_docs: Rob Pelinka | Robert Todd Pelinka Jr. (born December 23, 1969) is an American lawyer, National Basketball Association (NBA) team executive, sports agent, and former college basketball player from Lake Bluff, Illinois (suburban Chicago).  Pelinka is currently the general manager of the Los Angeles Lakers.

2016–17 Los Angeles Lakers season | The 2016–17 Los Angeles Lakers season was the franchise's 69th season, its 68th season in the National Basketball Association (NBA), and its 57th in Los Angeles.  It was also the first season without Kobe Bryant since the 1995–96 season.  It would also be the season where after multiple opportunities to improve upon themselves came and went, the Lakers decided to replace Jim Buss and Mitch Kupchak with former Lakers legend Magic Johnson and former sports agent Rob Pelinka on February 21, 2017 as both president of basketball operations and general manager respectively.  Furthermore, it was the season where Jeanie Buss would officially be named the primary owner of the Lakers on March 27.

List of Los Angeles Lakers head coaches | The Los Angeles Lakers are an American professional basketball team based in Los Angeles, California, formerly known as the Minneapolis Lakers from 1948 to 1960.  They play in the Pacific Division of the Western Conference in the National Basketball Association (NBA) The Lakers have played their home games at the Staples Center since 1999.  The franchise took its official name from Minnesota's nickname, the Land of 10,000 Lakes.  At the time the name was revealed, the Lakers were in Minneapolis.  In their franchise history, the team has only missed the NBA playoffs five times.  According to "Forbes" magazine, the Lakers are the second most valuable basketball franchise in the NBA, valued at approximately US$1 billion, surpassed only by the New York Knicks.  The Lakers are majority-owned by Jerry Buss's family trust, while Rob Pelinka is the general manager.

Anton Pelinka | Anton Pelinka (born October 14, 1941) is a professor of political science and nationalism studies at the English-speaking Central European University of Budapest.  Prior to this appointment, Pelinka was a professor of political science at the University of Innsbruck, one of Austria's largest universities.  During his career he has also served as a dean, with his most recent tenure in this role occurring between the years of 2004 an 2006 when he was dean of the Faculty of Political Science and Sociology at the University of Innsbruck.

Rob Babcock | Rob Babcock (born  1953 in Phoenix, Arizona) is the former Vice President of Basketball Operations with the Minnesota Timberwolves of the NBA.  He is also the former general manager of the Toronto Raptors.
Rob Hennigan | Rob Hennigan (born March 26, 1982) is an American basketball executive.  He is the former general manager of the Orlando Magic.  Hired at age 30, he was the youngest GM in the NBA.

Rob Lord (software developer) | Rob Lord is an American software executive best known as a founder of the Songbird media player application.  He also created the Internet Underground Music Archive and worked on Winamp as its general manager.
hop2_titles: List of Los Angeles Lakers head coaches
response.summary_2: Rob Pelinka is currently the general manager of the Los Angeles Lakers. Rob Pelinka is an American lawyer, former sports agent, and NBA team executive.
